Will said, "Really great projects often start with a simple public good. Just like Satoshi Nakamoto created Bitcoin, he did not pre-mine, did not raise funds, but created the most successful financial innovation in human history. ”

The dawn of the future

Several major events that occurred in 2025 are changing the rules of the game.

Circle’s listing was like a spark, lighting up stablecoins and RWAs (Real-World Assets).

This stablecoin issuer was listed on the New York Stock Exchange with a valuation of approximately US$4.5 billion, giving traditional VCs a long-awaited "non-tokenized" exit example. Subsequently, Bullish, Figure, and others went public one after another, giving more investors confidence.

“We don’t touch the primary and secondary markets of pure tokens, but we will look at stablecoins and RWAs,” said several traditional VCs. Investors offer the same assessment. The reasons are simple: ample room for growth, visible cash flow, and a clearer regulatory path. Stablecoins offer a more "bank-like" business model, with reserve fund spreads, issuance/redemption and settlement fees, and service fees from compliant custody and clearing networks, naturally creating the potential for sustainable profitability. RWAs, on the other hand, move receivables, government bonds, mortgages/real estate, and fund shares onto the blockchain, generating revenue from fees and spreads across multiple stages, including issuance/matching/custody/circulation. If the previous generation of crypto companies listed on the US stock market were primarily exchanges, mining companies, and asset management firms, then the new generation of prospectuses is dominated by stablecoins and RWAs. Meanwhile, the boundaries between stocks and tokens are blurring. The "MicroStrategy"-style treasury strategy has attracted a wave of imitators. Listed companies are allocating to leading assets like BTC/ETH/SOL through equity financing or PIPE issuances, transforming themselves into "crypto-equity." Behind the leaders of this sector are many classic VCs like Peter Tiel. Some institutions are even entering the market themselves, such as Huaxing Capital, which announced a $100 million investment in BNB, choosing to participate in crypto asset allocation through the open market. "The traditional financial world is embracing crypto," Wang Yuehua said. "Look at Nasdaq investing $50 million in Gemini. This is not only a capital move, but also a change in attitude. This shift is also reflected at the LP level. According to multiple interviewees, traditional LPs such as sovereign funds, pension funds, and university endowment funds are beginning to reassess the value of allocating to crypto assets. The past decade of capital has seen the ebb and flow of the tide. Asia's classic VCs once pushed exchanges onto the stage and shouted "All in" in unison during the bull market, but in the end they became marginal players in the crypto world. Although the current reality is bleak, the future may not be without hope. As Will firmly believes: "Classic VCs will definitely allocate more to crypto-related fintech investments." The future of classic VCs Will there be another large-scale entry? No one dares to assert. The only certainty is that the pace of progress in the crypto world will not stop.

data.title: Andrew Kang: Tom Lee's ETH investment thesis is ridiculous
data.description:
I recently came across Tom Lee's ETH investment thesis, which is possibly the most ridiculous collection of financial illiteracy I've ever encountered. Let's break it down one by one. Tom Lee's thesis is based on five key points: Stablecoins and the adoption of real-world asset tokens; the digital oil analogy; institutions will purchase and stake ETH to provide security for their tokenized asset networks and as operating capital; ETH's market capitalization will be equal to the combined value of all financial infrastructure companies; and technical analysis. 1. Stablecoins and the Adoption of Real-World Asset Tokenization: This argument suggests that the growth of stablecoins and asset tokenization activity should drive up trading volume, and in turn, drive up ETH fees/revenue. While this seems reasonable on the surface, a few minutes of data verification reveals otherwise.

Since 2020, the value of tokenized assets and stablecoin trading volume have increased 100-1000 times. Tom's argument fundamentally misunderstands the value accrual mechanism. It might lead you to believe that fees will scale year-over-year, but in reality, they remain roughly the same as in 2020. Here are the reasons: Ethereum network upgrades have improved transaction efficiency; stablecoin and tokenized asset activity has shifted to other chains; tokenizing low-liquidity assets generates almost no fees. Tokenized value isn't directly correlated with ETH revenue. Someone could tokenize $100 million in bonds. If they traded them every two years, how much would that translate into ETH fees? About $0.10? A single USDT transaction would generate significantly more in fees. Even if a trillion dollars in assets were tokenized, if their liquidity was low, it might only add $100,000 in ETH value. Will blockchain transaction volume and fees increase? Yes, but most of these fees will be captured by other blockchains with greater business expansion capabilities. Other competitors are actively seizing opportunities in the blockchain space for bringing traditional financial transactions to blockchains. Solana, Arbitrum, and Tempo have already achieved significant early success. Even Tether is supporting two new chains, Plasma and Stable, both of which aim to shift USDT trading volume to their own chains. 2. Digital Oil Analogy Oil is a commodity. Real, inflation-adjusted oil prices have fluctuated within the same range for over a century, with only periodic spikes and declines. I agree with Tom's view of ETH as a commodity, but that's not a positive development. I'm not sure what Tom is trying to argue here! 3. Institutions will purchase and stake ETH to provide security for their asset tokenization networks and as operating capital. Have large banks and other financial institutions already allocated ETH on their balance sheets? No.

Has any organization announced any plans to do so? No.

Will banks stockpile gasoline due to continued energy consumption? No, because the proportion is too small, they can simply purchase it when needed.

Will banks purchase shares in the asset custodians they use? No.

4. ETH's market capitalization will be equal to the combined value of all financial infrastructure companies

My point is, this once again exposes a fundamental misunderstanding of the value accumulation mechanism and is pure delusion.

5. Technical Analysis

I'm actually a big believer in technical analysis and believe its objective application is extremely valuable. Unfortunately, Tom seems to be using technical analysis to arbitrarily draw lines to support his biases. Looking objectively at this chart, the most striking observation is that Ethereum is in a multi-year range. This is similar to the wide range-bound behavior of crude oil prices over the past three decades. Not only are we range-bound, but we recently hit the top of the range and failed to break through resistance. If anything, the technical picture for Ethereum is bearish. I cannot rule out the possibility of a prolonged period of consolidation between $1,000 and $4,800. Past parabolic rallies by no means guarantee their indefinite continuation. The long-term ETH/BTC chart is also misinterpreted. The pair is indeed in a multi-year range, but has been primarily constrained by a downtrend over the past few years, with only a recent bounce off long-term support. This downward trend is driven by a saturated narrative surrounding Ethereum and an inability of fundamentals to support valuation growth. These fundamentals remain unchanged. Ethereum's valuation stems primarily from the effects of financial illiteracy. To be fair, this can create significant market capitalization (see XRP). However, the valuations that financial illiteracy can support are not infinite. Macro liquidity is keeping ETH's market capitalization afloat, but unless significant organizational change occurs, it is likely destined for continued underperformance.

data.title: Can ASTER truly challenge Hyperliquid's dominance?
data.description:
Original Title: The Aster CounterstrikeAuthor: Token DispatchCompiled & Translated by: LenaXin, ChainCatcher

Background

On September 18, 2025, Zhao Changpeng published a price chart that wasn't Bitcoin or BNB.

The chart represented the native token of ASTER, a decentralized perpetual exchange that had just launched the previous day.

"Great job! Great start. Keep up the good work!" he said.

Within hours, the ASTER token soared 400% from its issue price. The market clearly received the signal: the former Binance CEO wasn't just offering congratulations; he was declaring war on Hyperliquid. As HYPE token holders watched their Hyperliquid assets soar to an all-time high of nearly $60, Changpeng Zhao's carefully calculated tweet landed like a pinpoint strike. While banned from operating Binance, Changpeng Zhao clearly wasn't barred from shaking up the market; he was fully supporting Hyperliquid's strongest competitor. However, the war machine had already begun. YZi Labs (formerly Binance Labs) had been quietly funding the development of ASTER. The BNB Chain partnership network was activating. The world's largest crypto empire was launching an all-out campaign against this decentralized upstart that dared to seize market share in the derivatives market. This was the opening salvo in the war for decentralized exchanges in perpetual contracts, and Binance had no intention of conceding. (1) What is ASTER? ASTER was born from the December 2024 merger of two major DeFi protocols, Astherus and APX Finance. This integration created a unified trading infrastructure designed to directly compete with Hyperliquid's growing market share. (Note: Astherus focuses on multi-asset liquidity for yield-generating products, while APX Finance is a decentralized perpetual trading platform.) The platform is a multi-chain decentralized exchange that supports the BNB chain, Ethereum, Solana, and Arbitrum networks. By aggregating multi-chain liquidity, it enables seamless trading without manual cross-chain transactions. Since its launch in March 2025, ASTER has processed over US$514 billion in trading volume for 2 million users. After the token issuance, the platform's total locked-in value briefly peaked at $2 billion, but by September 2025, it had fallen back to $655 million. Unlike decentralized exchanges that focus solely on spot trading, ASTER is positioned as a comprehensive trading platform offering both spot and perpetual futures markets. While perpetual derivatives are its core business, the platform also offers spot trading, with the first trading pair to be launched being its native token, ASTER/USDT. (II) How does ASTER address front-running and liquidation manipulation? ASTER's core architecture is dedicated to addressing the liquidity fragmentation problem plaguing multi-chain DeFi. The platform builds "unified liquidity" by aggregating cross-chain order books, rather than forcing users to transfer assets across chains. Its "hidden order" system is particularly notable, concealing the order size and direction until execution. This dark pool-like design effectively addresses key pain points of on-chain trading: front-running and liquidation manipulation. Zhao Changpeng previously commented on this feature, stating that it addresses "the liquidation manipulation issues seen in other on-chain DEXes." The platform's margin system supports both segregated and cross-collateralized trading, allowing users to use liquid collateral tokens like BNB or ecosystem stablecoins as collateral. This capital-efficient innovation allows traders to maintain active positions while earning passive income on their collateralized assets. The core of the ASTER ecosystem is USDF, an ecosystem stablecoin backed by a delta-neutral position. Users can mint USDF by depositing supported assets and use the stablecoin as collateral to earn returns. This creates a self-circulating liquidity system in which stablecoin holders naturally become liquidity providers. The ASTER roadmap includes the integration of zero-knowledge proofs for enhanced privacy and the development of Aster Chain, a customized Layer 1 blockchain optimized for trading. This feature was released in beta to a limited number of traders in June 2025, enabling the platform to eventually compete with Hyperliquid's customized Layer 1 architecture. (III) The Ultimate Showdown between ASTER and Hyperliquid ASTER and Hyperliquid represent two distinct approaches to decentralized perpetual contract trading. Hyperliquid built its own dedicated Layer 1 blockchain from the ground up, utilizing a fully on-chain order book, achieving performance comparable to that of centralized exchanges. This vertical integration has made Hyperliquiquis an industry benchmark for transaction execution efficiency and user experience, but it also limits it to a single ecosystem. ASTER takes the opposite approach, maximizing coverage and liquidity access through a multi-chain deployment. While this increases technical complexity, it allows it to leverage the existing DeFi ecosystem and serve user groups with a preference for specific chains. Hyperliquid dominates the DeFi perpetual swap market with approximately 70% of the market share, with open interest reaching $15 billion and an average daily trading volume consistently exceeding $800 million. However, ASTER's multi-chain strategy offers advantages that Hyperliquid struggles to replicate. The platform's integration with yield protocols like Pendle and Venus creates capital efficiency opportunities that Hyperliquid's isolated L1 architecture cannot achieve. ASTER users can simultaneously earn BNB staking rewards, USDT deposit interest, and trading fees. Leverage configurations also vary significantly. Hyperliquid caps leverage at 40x, while ASTER supports 100x leverage for most trading pairs, with specific assets reaching up to 1001x. ASTER's US stock perpetual contracts provide 24/7 exposure to traditional stock market risks, expanding its service reach beyond crypto-native traders. (IV) Token Flows Reveal the Competitive Landscape ASTER's token economics model prioritizes community incentives and long-term sustainability. The total supply of 8 billion tokens is allocated according to a specific ratio: 53.5% for airdrops and community rewards, 30% for ecosystem development, 7% for the treasury, 5% for the team, and 4.5% for liquidity and exchange listings. The community allocation ratio is among the highest in the DeFi space, exceeding 50% of the total supply. The unlocking mechanism involves releasing 25% of tokens immediately upon generation, with the remaining tokens released linearly over nine months after a three-month lock-up period. This token has multiple functions within the ecosystem, including governance rights, fee discounts, staking income, and access to premium features. Revenue sharing is achieved through fee buybacks. A portion of transaction fees will be used to purchase and potentially burn ASTER tokens, creating deflationary pressure as trading volume grows. Users can stake their ASTER tokens and use their ecosystem derivatives as trading collateral. This allows a single position to generate multiple value streams. (V) Token Showdown: A Comprehensive Analysis of ASTER and HYPE The economic models of the ASTER and HYPE tokens demonstrate different philosophies of value capture and distribution mechanisms. Hyperliquid's HYPE token adopts a more traditional cryptoeconomic model, with aggressive buybacks using protocol revenue. The platform has annualized revenue exceeding $1 billion and uses the majority of its revenue for HYPE buybacks, creating strong deflationary pressure. HYPE's core advantage lies in its proven "revenue-buyback" flywheel. Currently, 43.4% of the total supply is staked, and this, combined with substantial protocol revenue, maintains a strict circulating supply limit. This powerful price support mechanism is currently unmatched by ASTER. In November 2025, Hyperliquid will initiate a large-scale token unlock for core contributors. These unlocks will generate significant selling pressure, potentially overwhelming even aggressive buyback plans. Hyperliquid is preparing to launch the USDH stablecoin to generate additional buyback pressure, but the timing introduces uncertainty. ASTER's strategy prioritizes community ownership over immediate value capture. While this may result in weaker short-term buybacks, it creates stronger network effects and decentralized governance. The 53.5% community allocation ensures that value truly flows to actual users, not early investors or team members. (VI) Binance's Strategy An analysis of ASTER's liquidity provision system reveals the strategic depth behind its launch. Professional market makers provide core order book depth, while the platform's cross-chain architecture aggregates liquidity from multiple blockchains. Strategic partnerships with protocols such as Pendle, ListaDAO, Kernel, Venus, YieldNest, and PancakeSwap create additional sources of liquidity and user incentives. Pendle tokenizes yield, ListaDAO provides BNB staking liquidity (generating asBNB), Venus offers lending services, and PancakeSwap directs arbitrage trading from the largest DEX on the BNB chain. These partnerships make ASTER a core hub where users can participate in multiple DeFi strategies while trading derivatives. The ecosystem collateral system addresses the opportunity cost issue by allowing users to earn returns from their trading margin. Users no longer need to hold idle USDT; instead, they can mint USDF as collateral while earning a delta-neutral return. (Note: USDF is ASTER's ecosystem stablecoin.) Similarly, asBNB can still earn staking returns (approximately 5-7% annualized) when used as collateral for leveraged positions. This allows a single deposit to generate multiple income streams: staking income, trading profits, and token rewards. This incentivizes users to retain more funds on the platform long-term, naturally deepening the liquidity pool. YZi Lab's investment timeline provides a key footnote to Binance's strategic layout. The investment firm completed its investment in Astherus (the predecessor of ASTER) in November 2024, just as Hyperliquid posed a substantial competitive threat to Binance's dominant derivatives market. According to a BNB Chain representative, ASTER, as a key project in the YZi Lab incubation program, received mentorship, ecosystem exposure, and technical and marketing resources, solidifying its position as the leading perpetual contract DEX on the BNB Chain. Hyperliquid's trading volume continued to grow steadily between 2024 and 2025. While Binance maintains a significant advantage in absolute trading volume, Hyperliquid's growth trajectory from near-zero to significant market share demonstrates its success in carving out an independent market rather than directly competing with Binance traders. Combined with its investments in other projects like MYXFinance, YZi Labs' investment strategy is becoming increasingly clear. These investments demonstrate a coordinated effort to build DeFi infrastructure on the BNB chain and create alternatives to successful protocols on other chains. (Note: MYXFinance is a perpetual contract on the BNB chain, and its DEX has also experienced explosive growth.) Binance's overall strategy focuses more on ecosystem defense than direct competition. Rather than simply copying Hyperliquid's customized L1 solution, it leverages the advantages of its existing ecosystem: regulatory relationships, fiat currency access, institutional partnerships, and deep liquidity pools. ASTER, while capitalizing on these network effects, is providing a decentralized trading experience increasingly favored by sophisticated traders. Rather than viewing decentralized protocols as a threat to be ignored or marginalized, Binance actively invests in and promotes DeFi alternatives that remain within its ecosystem's sphere of influence. (VII) Conclusion? The rise of ASTER either marks a turning point for decentralized derivatives trading or becomes Changpeng Zhao's most expensive "I can do this" statement to his competitors. On the surface, the platform possesses all the elements of success: multi-chain liquidity, ecosystem collateral, perpetual US stock contracts, and strong backing. The idea of ​​trading derivatives while earning returns sounds appealing, but it's important to note that innovations in the cryptocurrency world that seem too good to be true often are. The fact that total locked value has plummeted from a peak of $2 billion to $655 million may serve as a warning: there's a fundamental difference between initial enthusiasm and sustained adoption. When TVL plummets 67% in a single day, we might question whether these figures represent real users or just those driven by quick-fix yield farmers. Its token economics prioritize long-term community building over short-term value capture, a strategy that can be considered either visionary or naive, depending on perspective. Unlike Hyperliquid's proven "revenue-buyback" machine, ASTER's value proposition requires users to believe in the sustainability of a business model that earns a 3% margin yield on 100x leveraged positions. The true test will be whether the platform can convince traders to abandon Hyperliquid's proven infrastructure for multi-chain experiments supported by the ecosystem that nurtured the 2022 FTX crash. When the world's largest exchange feels compelled to support a DeFi competitor, it suggests that the centralized model isn't as unshakable as once believed. Whether this move will make ASTER a winner or just an expensive risk hedge remains to be seen.

data.title: Stablecoin Data Panorama in 2025: Growth, Application and New Regulation
data.description:
Digital assets combine the stability of traditional currencies with the flexibility of blockchain technology—this is no longer a dream, but a rapidly developing reality. From central banks to startups, the stablecoin ecosystem has grown into a cornerstone of the digital economy. This article uses a data perspective to analyze key milestones and cutting-edge developments in stablecoins, highlighting their impact and future trajectory. Key Data Summary By mid-2025, the total market capitalization of stablecoins reached $251.7 billion. They are actively used in over 50 countries, particularly in regions with high inflation or limited banking services, such as Nigeria, Argentina, and Vietnam. Tether (USDT) continues to lead the stablecoin market, with a circulating supply of approximately $112 billion, accounting for approximately 68% of the total stablecoin market capitalization. It processes approximately $20-25 billion in daily trading volume. With regulatory support, USD Coin (USDC)'s market position is expected to further consolidate in 2025. Dai (DAI), with approximately $6.7 billion in circulation, maintains its leading position among decentralized stablecoins. Stablecoins are increasingly used in Ethereum transactions, strengthening their role in DeFi. Growth Drivers and Core Use Cases: Stablecoin adoption is projected to increase significantly in 2025, with a Chainalysis report showing higher year-over-year growth in emerging markets. Stablecoins are increasingly used for cross-border transfers, with some platforms reporting over 50% of their remittance volume driven by stablecoins. Stablecoin remittance service fees are 30-60% lower, with a global average of approximately 2-3%, compared to 6% for traditional bank remittances. Retail adoption is surging, with consumers using stablecoins for e-commerce payments, with fees as low as 0.1%, far lower than the 3.5% charged by credit cards. Central banks around the world continue to explore stablecoin integration, and the US has introduced regulatory frameworks such as the GENIUS Act. By 2025, tokenized real-world assets (RWA) will exceed $10 billion, with stablecoins becoming the primary settlement layer. By 2025, DeFi's total value locked (TVL) will reach $123.6 billion, with stablecoins contributing approximately 40%. Stablecoin Interest Rates and On-Chain Distribution Ethereum dominates the stablecoin landscape, holding 65.4% of the total supply.

  • Base, an emerging public chain, accounts for 5.6%.

  • Arbitrum, BNB Chain, and Solana each account for 3.7%.

  • Sonic accounts for 3.1%, Polygon and Avalanche account for 2.5% and 1.9% respectively. In terms of yield, 42% of stablecoin pools have an annualized yield (APY) of less than 5%; approximately 32.1% of pools offer an APY of 5-10%, which is the most common medium-yield range; 21% of pools offer a higher APY of 10-15%; and only 4.9% of pools offer an APY premium of more than 15%.

    （Data source: Datawallet）

    Centralized Stablecoins

    • Tether (USDT) maintains its dominant position with over $112 billion in circulation and approximately 68% market share.

    • Circle's USDC reached a market share of 24.3% in the first quarter of 2025, with a market capitalization of $64 billion, and increased institutional support. Pax Dollar (USDP) circulation is estimated to reach $1.3 billion in 2025, with continued growth in adoption. Binance USD (BUSD) market concentration is increasing, with circulation falling to approximately $4 billion in 2025. Traditional financial integration is deepening, with Visa and Mastercard supporting stablecoin payments on over 10 global networks. By mid-2025, centralized stablecoins will account for approximately 90% of the total stablecoin market. Approximately 12 countries will recognize centralized stablecoins for cross-border trade in 2025. Decentralized Stablecoins: Decentralized stablecoin market share is projected to increase from 18% in 2023 to 20% in 2025. Dai (DAI) will surpass $10 billion in market capitalization in 2025, leading decentralized stablecoins. Reserve Protocol (RSV) has approximately $500 million in circulation, driven primarily by adoption in Latin America. Security enhancements on platforms like MakerDAO have reduced stablecoin-related vulnerabilities by 30%.

    • The steady growth of liquid, collateralized derivative stablecoins such as LUSD reflects broader DeFi integration.

    Global participation has significantly expanded user engagement on decentralized stablecoin platforms. The collateralization ratio of decentralized models remains healthy at approximately 160%.

    Market Capitalization and Distribution

    The total stablecoin market capitalization reached a record high of $252 billion in mid-2025. The five major stablecoins, USDT, USDC, DAI, BUSD, and TUSD, account for approximately 90% of the total market capitalization. Decentralized stablecoins such as DAI and FRAX account for 20% of the market capitalization.

    • Ethereum remains the leading stablecoin holder, accounting for approximately 70% of the supply.

    • BNB Chain ranks second, accounting for approximately 15%.

    Stablecoins are used in DeFi applications for approximately $60 billion, highlighting their strong utility. Regional adoption in Asia and Africa contributes nearly 50% of global stablecoin trading volume. Transaction Volume and User Adoption: Stablecoins are expected to process over $5.7 trillion in transactions in 2024, with a 66% surge in Q1 2025. Over 80% of stablecoin activity is concentrated on the Ethereum and BNB Chain networks. Stablecoins account for 40% of total trading volume on crypto exchanges. The number of global stablecoin wallet addresses exceeds 500 million, with emerging markets experiencing 30% annual growth. Corporate use of stablecoins is expected to grow by approximately 25%, particularly in cross-border payments and supply chain settlements. (The average stablecoin remittance fee has been reduced to 2.5%, compared to 5% for traditional bank remittances.) Over 25,000 merchants worldwide accept stablecoins for online transactions. USDC Stablecoin Reserve Composition Cash and government money market funds account for 61% of reserves, ensuring high liquidity and stability. Yankee CDs account for 13%, diversified through international bank deposits. U.S. Treasuries account for 12%, reflecting a reliance on safe government bonds. Commercial paper accounts for 9%, providing exposure to short-term corporate debt. Corporate bonds account for 5%. Municipal bonds account for just 0.2%.

    • (Data source: Swyftx Learn)

    Regulatory Dynamics and Compliance

    As of mid-2025, 12 countries and regions, including the United States, the United Kingdom, Singapore, and Hong Kong, have introduced dedicated regulatory frameworks for stablecoins. Ten central banks have announced collaborations with stablecoin providers to achieve cross-border central bank digital currency (CBDC) interoperability.

    Approximately 80% of top stablecoin projects comply with AML and KYC standards. Approximately 90% of stablecoin projects regularly publish reserve audit reports, enhancing issuer transparency. The European Union's Markets in Crypto-Assets (MiCA) Directive has fully come into effect, setting compliance standards for stablecoin issuers in its member states. The US GENIUS Act, passed in July 2025, establishes a federal licensing and compliance framework, requiring 1:1 asset backing, audits, and reserve transparency. Hong Kong's new regulations, effective May 2025, will require stablecoin issuers to be licensed. Stablecoin Usage Distribution: DeFi and trading accounted for 67% of total stablecoin activity, dominating the market. Remittances accounted for 15%, highlighting their role in cross-border transfers. Inflation hedging accounted for 10%, used for wealth preservation in unstable economies. Merchant payments accounted for 5% , reflecting its gradual adoption in retail and commerce. Other uses accounted for 3% .

    (Data source: Econofact)

    Academic Research and Literature

    Over 30% of crypto-related content on mainstream financial blogs and journals involves stablecoins. Over 50% of blockchain-related books include dedicated chapters on stablecoins.

    Academic publications on stablecoins increased by 18%, with over 200 new research papers expected in 2025. The number of white papers on algorithmic stablecoins tripled, focusing on hybrid decentralized collateral models. A 2024 Harvard Business Review study found that 60% of institutional investors favor stablecoins; the MIT Digital Currency Initiative continues to emphasize the role of stablecoins in promoting financial inclusion; and Stanford University has established a Stablecoin and Digital Asset Lab to promote regulatory innovation. Recent Developments: Stablecoins have been integrated into the FedNow system, providing near-instant settlement for US businesses. Amazon has launched a pilot program to accept stablecoins for global e-commerce transactions; Mastercard has expanded stablecoin support to select merchants and integrated it into its existing card-based infrastructure, which covers over 150 million merchants; and BlackRock has included stablecoins in its portfolio, demonstrating institutional confidence. Ethereum's Shanghai upgrade doubled the scalability and processing speed of stablecoins. Ripple's liquidity hub expanded stablecoin support to accelerate cross-border payments. Furthermore, a pilot program for blockchain payments in rural India saw a 60% increase in stablecoin trading volume. Conclusion: The stablecoin market has solidified its position as a cornerstone of the digital economy, bridging traditional finance and blockchain. From cross-border payments to DeFi and even the integration of institutional finance, stablecoins are no longer niche tools but a vital part of global financial infrastructure. With technological advancements and clearer regulations, stablecoins will undoubtedly continue to play a key role in shaping the future global financial ecosystem.

data.title: FTX Founder Behind Bars Causes FTT Token to Soar 60% in 12 Minutes With Single X Post: How Much Influence Does He Still Wield After Conviction?
data.description:
Sam Bankman-Fried Sparks FTT Surge With Simple “Gm” Despite Prison Restrictions

Sam Bankman-Fried, the former CEO of bankrupt exchange FTX, has once again captured attention despite serving a 25-year federal sentence.

On 23 September, his X account, @SBF_FTX, posted a single “gm” message, shorthand for “good morning.”

The post quickly drew reactions across the crypto space, ranging from disbelief to sarcasm.

Influencer Peter McCormack simply wrote:

“What.”

Wendy O quipped:

“Maybe the rapture is real?”

While @CryptoVonDoom urged:

“GO BACK TO YOUR CELL.”

GO BACK TO YOUR CELL pic.twitter.com/8yNdrMNOES

— VonDoom ☕️ (@CryptoVonDoom) September 23, 2025

Analyst Conor Grogan commented:

“Here comes the PR campaign to get Sam pardoned!”

Three hours later, the account clarified that SBF did not personally make the post, which was handled by a friend.

GO BACK TO YOUR CELL pic.twitter.com/8yNdrMNOES

— VonDoom ☕️ (@CryptoVonDoom) September 23, 2025

His X bio notes that it is “shared by a friend” and lists a monitored prison mailing address at FCI Terminal Island in California.

Despite these restrictions, activity on the account continues sporadically, previously including cryptic threads about management and responsibility.

FTT Token Sees Unusual Rally Following Post

The brief X activity had a striking impact on FTX’s native token, FTT.

Following the post, the price surged by over 60% in just 12 minutes, peaking at an intraday high of more than $1.20 before settling around $1.

JUST IN: FTX's $FTT token just skyrocketed 60% in 12 minutes after Sam Bankman-Fried (@SBF_FTX) said "GM" pic.twitter.com/PKkcR0DKn9

— BlockNews (@blocknewsdotcom) September 24, 2025

Volume jumped nearly sixfold, hitting $66.6 million within 24 hours.

Analysts noted the volatility as unusual given the token lost its utility following FTX’s 2022 collapse, having previously been used for exchange discounts and collateral.

Despite the bankruptcy, renewed interest in FTT highlights lingering community speculation.

The account began mass-following other profiles, including Web3 developers, Solana accounts, automated news reposting accounts, and users advocating for a pardon.

FTX Bankruptcy Creditors Set For $1.6 Billion Distribution

Amid the social media stir, FTX creditors are preparing for another round of repayments.

The FTX Recovery Trust announced that $1.6 billion will be distributed starting 30 September, routed through BitGo, Kraken, and Payoneer.

FTX is set to dispense its third wave of bankruptcy repayments this month! 💸 The FTX Recovery Trust will distribute a total of $1.6 billion to creditors on September 30. A step toward closure for clients of the exchange that went bankrupt in 2022. #FTX#CryptoBankruptcypic.twitter.com/vcBKoPC8X5

— NameCoinNews (@NameCoinNews_) September 22, 2025

U.S. customers will receive 40%, lifting total recoveries to 95%, while international “dotcom” customers gain 6%, bringing cumulative payouts to 78%.

Unsecured and loan claims rise to 85%, and convenience claims exceed face value at 120%.

This marks the third major distribution, bringing total returns to more than $6 billion since the exchange’s collapse in November 2022.

FTX Recovery Trust Files $1.15 Billion Lawsuit Against Genesis Digital Assets

The bankruptcy estate is also pursuing legal action to recover additional funds.

On 22 September, the FTX Recovery Trust filed a $1.15 billion lawsuit against Bitcoin mining firm Genesis Digital Assets.

Breaking: FTX Recovery Trust Sues Genesis Digital Assets to Claw Back $1.15 Billion in Allegedly Fraudulent Transfers

Delaware, Sept. 23, 2025 — The FTX Recovery Trust has filed a lawsuit against Bitcoin mining giant Genesis Digital Assets (GDA), seeking to recover $1.15 billion… pic.twitter.com/jP1iDTKQd0

— PAXTIBI LLP (@PAXTIBI_xyz) September 23, 2025

The complaint alleges that Genesis received preferential payments from Alameda Research, with $550.9 million allegedly sent directly to GDA co-founders Rashit Makhat and Marco Krohn.

Why SBF Remains Behind Bars

SBF’s prison term follows his 2023 conviction on seven counts, including fraud and conspiracy.

Prosecutors cited billions in misused customer funds for risky Alameda Research bets, political donations, and a lavish lifestyle.

The federal court handed down a 25-year sentence, one of the most severe in crypto history, citing the extensive deception and harm to millions of users.

Sam Bankman-Fried sentenced to 25 years in prison

March 28, 2024https://t.co/QjHtZjLEZ2pic.twitter.com/dWSXtpFa9d

— web3 is going just great (@web3isgreat) March 28, 2024

Federal prison regulations strictly prohibit internet and social media access, allowing inmates only monitored communication through Trulincs, a text-only messaging system.

Is Crypto Confidence Detached From Reality?

Coinlive observes that despite SBF’s incarceration and the collapse of FTX, community reaction to a single social media post shows how sentiment can drive market movements, detached from fundamentals.

The FTT token’s sudden surge, fueled by speculation rather than utility or corporate recovery, raises questions about the psychology of crypto investors and the fragility of trust in digital assets.

It prompts reflection on whether market excitement is grounded in fact or simply the allure of past personalities, even behind bars.
